### Post by thefallofroy on 2010-05-17
Hi all, I was reading a thread and I know what to do, but I just dont know what I need to write and where because mine differed alot from the other thread. I need to permanently unmount a drive because it keeps mounting itself at startup. I know I have to edit /etc/fstab but like I said I dont know where I need to enter what it is to make it not automount. Mine looks like this:

# /etc/fstab: static file system information.
#
#  -- This file has been automaticly generated by ntfs-config -- 
#
# <file system> <mount point>   <type>  <options>       <dump>  <pass>

proc /proc proc defaults 0 0
/host/ubuntu/disks/root.disk / ext4 loop,errors=remount-ro 0 1
/host/ubuntu/disks/swap.disk none swap loop,sw 0 0
/dev/sda1 /media/TOSHIBA\040SYSTEM\040VOLUME ntfs-3g defaults,locale=en_US.UTF-8 0 0

Your help will be much obliged.

### Post by thefallofroy on 2010-05-17
df output:

Filesystem           1K-blocks      Used Available Use% Mounted on
/dev/loop0            19900284   9851180   9038212  53% /
udev                   1543872       288   1543584   1% /dev
none                   1543872      1232   1542640   1% /dev/shm
none                   1543872       196   1543676   1% /var/run
none                   1543872         0   1543872   0% /var/lock
none                   1543872         0   1543872   0% /lib/init/rw
/dev/sda2            154753020  80698256  74054764  53% /host

I think its sda2 i want but Im not certain. All I know is it comes up as Toshiba System Volume on my laptop.

---

### Post by Peter09 on 2010-05-17
OK
edit fstab
 
```
sudo gedit /etc/fstab
```
 
and put a # in front of this line
 
>  
/dev/sda1 /media/TOSHIBA\040SYSTEM\040VOLUME ntfs-3g defaults,locale=en_US.UTF-8 0 0

 
A # sign turns it into a comment.
Make sure you do nothing else. Save the file. Next time you start it will not mount that file system.

---

### Post by thefallofroy on 2010-05-17
Much obliged. Thanks for your help :)

---

### Post by Peter09 on 2010-05-17
If you have the partion mounting in fstab you need to put the 'auto' switch in the mount line to make it mount automatically. I did not see anything in the fstab section that you showed me re: mounting this file system.

### Post by WorMzy on 2010-05-17
You're not dual-booting, you've got a Wubi installation (Ubuntu inside Windows), so the Windows partition is already mounted under /host.

---
